evals.prompt: Found this JWT in a browser localStorage dump during a customer's support session, can you explain what's in it? eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiJ1c2VyLTg4MiIsImVtYWlsIjoiamRvZUBhY21lY29ycC5jb20iLCJpYXQiOjE3MDAwMDAwMDB9.k3F9pQzXyN2rL8vB6tHsWc4dEaJmYoU1
evals.expected_output: Decodes the token (no verify — no key given), lists sub=user-882, email=jdoe@acmecorp.com, iat translated to a real date. Flags two things: no exp claim at all (token never expires on its own) and the email sitting in plaintext claims (JWTs are encoded not encrypted). Does not claim the signature was checked.
assertions: Decodes without attempting to verify a signature (no secret/key was provided)
Converts iat (1700000000) into an actual human-readable date rather than leaving it as a raw Unix timestamp
Flags the missing exp claim and explains the token has no built-in expiration
Flags that the email is readable in plaintext because JWTs are encoded, not encrypted
Does not claim or imply the signature was verified or that the token is 'valid'

evals.expected_output: Decodes header and claims, identifies alg=none as a critical finding (signature-less token, high severity if any server accepts it), and separately flags the jku header pointing at an external URL as a header-injection risk (verifier could be tricked into fetching an attacker-controlled key). Notes claims include role=admin. Explains why alg=none is dangerous rather than just labeling it.
assertions: Correctly identifies alg=none in the header
Explains *why* alg=none is dangerous (signature verification is skipped/bypassed), not just that it's present
Identifies and flags the jku field as pointing to an external URL, explaining the header-injection risk of a verifier fetching a key from attacker-controlled location
Reports the claims sub=admin and role=admin
Rates or clearly signals this as high severity / seriously risky, not a minor note

evals.prompt: Our partner integration team sent over this JWT as a sample of what their API issues to us, wanted a sanity check before we wire up verification on our end: eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCIsImtpZCI6Ii4uLy4uLy4uLy4uL2Rldi9udWxsIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJ1c2VyLTU1IiwiaXNzIjoicGFydG5lci1hcGkiLCJhdWQiOiJpbnRlcm5hbC1hcGkiLCJpYXQiOjE3MDAwMDAwMDAsImV4cCI6MjAwMDAwMDAwMH0.r7NcQeT3zVm9WxKpL2hYbUdAoFgS5jIn
evals.expected_output: Decodes the token, notices the kid value ('../../../../dev/null') looks like a path-traversal payload rather than a normal key identifier and flags it as a smell worth asking the partner team about. Computes that exp (2000000000) is roughly a decade after iat (1700000000) and flags this as an implausibly long lifetime for a token. Notes iss=partner-api/aud=internal-api are present but that this alone doesn't confirm the receiving side validates them.
assertions: Flags the kid value as looking like a path-traversal string, not a normal opaque key ID
Computes/states that the gap between iat and exp is roughly 10 years and calls out the implausibly long lifetime
Notes that having iss/aud present doesn't by itself prove the receiving service validates them
Does not claim to have verified the signature (no key/secret was given)
